sources.title: CALIPER Hematology Reference Standards (I): Improving Laboratory Test Interpretation in Children (Beckman Coulter DxH 900)
sources.organization: American Journal of Clinical Pathology (Oxford University Press)
limitations: Reference intervals are analyzer-specific (Beckman Coulter DxH 900) and not directly transferable to other hematology platforms
CALIPER cohort is Canadian; ethnic/geographic transferability not established here
Per-parameter numeric reference-interval tables not verbatim-verifiable from retrieved text
Neonatal (first days of life) dynamics not resolved beyond the 0-<1 y partition

This paper (DOI 10.1093/ajcp/aqaa059) was carded twice by the rf pipeline under distinct source_card_ids — src_20260718_rfcbc001_01 (trust.source_rank=primary, cites clm_009/clm_011) and src_20260718_rfcbc001_10 (trust.source_rank=secondary, cites clm_026/clm_027). Both are merged into this single evidence.json source (matching the sourceId alias modules/cbc_suite_v1/evidence-assertions.json already uses for both cards, 02 doc section 4.7's stable-identity rule for Source); the source_rank discrepancy between the two cards is preserved here, not resolved or averaged. rfSourceCardId records src_20260718_rfcbc001_01 as the canonical card; src_20260718_rfcbc001_10 is the duplicate.
conflictsWith: Adult hematology reference intervals (source states pediatric-specific standards are required, not adult).
supports: Age-specific reference-interval differences were statistically significant for 39 of 47 hematologic parameters, confirming pediatric intervals cannot rely on adult cutoffs (clm_009; direct basis for dec_cbc_local_range_precedence_001).

Establishes a database of pediatric reference standards for 47 hematologic parameters calculated per CLSI guidelines on the Beckman Coulter DxH 900 (clm_011; supports the cross-analyzer-portability inference clm_inf07).

All 47 hematologic parameters were measured on the Beckman Coulter DxH 900, making the reference standards analyzer/method-specific (clm_026; supports the cross-analyzer-portability inference clm_inf07).

Significant age-specific differences were observed for 39 of the 47 hematologic parameters, motivating age-partitioned reference intervals (clm_027; direct basis for dec_cbc_local_range_precedence_001).
sources.surveillanceQuery: CALIPER pediatric hematology reference intervals (Beckman Coulter DxH 900) update after 2020
sources.recencyNote: Retained per module.json evidence_policy.older_source_rule ("foundational_not_superseded_with_reason"): published 2020-08 is outside the module's 1825-day (five-year) recent-source window (evidence_policy.recent_window_days), but it is the RF-CBC-001 fixture's sole grounding for clm_009/clm_011/clm_026/clm_027 (age-partitioned, analyzer-specific reference-interval evidence), all cited by dec_cbc_local_range_precedence_001; no more recent equivalent-scope study is present in this bundle.

sources.title: Comprehensive pediatric reference intervals for 79 hematology markers in the CALIPER cohort of healthy children and adolescents using the Mindray BC-6800Plus system
limitations: Analyzer-specific (Mindray BC-6800Plus) — RIs are method-dependent and not directly transferable to other hematology analyzers
Canadian CALIPER cohort — population-specific
Numeric reference-interval values reside in paywalled full-text tables, not retrievable here
supports: 79 hematology parameters were assayed on the Mindray BC-6800Plus system — a separate analyzer from the DxH 900 CALIPER cohort — establishing that pediatric hematology reference intervals are analyzer-specific and not portable across platforms without local verification (clm_062; supports the cross-analyzer-portability inference clm_inf07).

sources.title: The diversity of mutations and clinical outcomes for ELANE-associated neutropenia
limitations: ANC threshold passages render the ×10^9/l exponent as '10/l' in the PMC text-extraction (superscript lost); numeric coefficient (0.2, 0.5, 2.0) is intact
Registry cohort not population-representative; diagnosis-assignment methodology may bias genotype-phenotype associations vs other series
supports: Thirty of 187 severe congenital neutropenia (SCN) patients (16%) developed MDS/AML versus 0% of cyclic neutropenia patients (p<0.0001); every frame-shift ELANE mutation occurred in the SCN group, concentrating myeloid-malignancy risk in the persistent congenital pattern (clm_053; direct basis for dec_cbc_marrow_red_flag_001).

Severe congenital neutropenia patients usually have an ANC below 0.5x10^9/L without regular cyclic fluctuations and a bone marrow showing myeloid maturation arrest (clm_051; supports the unit-normalization inference clm_inf01).
sources.surveillanceQuery: ELANE-associated congenital neutropenia mutation/outcome review update after 2015
sources.recencyNote: Retained per module.json evidence_policy.older_source_rule ("foundational_not_superseded_with_reason"): published 2015-01 is outside the module's 1825-day (five-year) recent-source window (evidence_policy.recent_window_days), but it is the RF-CBC-001 fixture's sole grounding for clm_053 (SCN vs cyclic-neutropenia MDS/AML incidence), cited directly by dec_cbc_marrow_red_flag_001; no more recent equivalent-scope study is present in this bundle.

sources.title: Stable Long-Term Risk of Leukaemia in Patients with Severe Congenital Neutropenia Maintained on G-CSF Therapy
authors: Severe Chronic Neutropenia International Registry (SCNIR) investigators
limitations: Full-text extraction from PMC dropped scientific-notation superscripts (e.g. '0.5 ×10/l' should read 0.5 ×10^9/L) — verified against standard SCN diagnostic convention but flag on ingest.
Cohort not age-stratified; SCN is congenital (dx typically infancy/childhood) but this report does not break out pediatric-only rates.
Risk estimates are cohort-level hazards, not individual-patient decision rules.
conflictsWith: Prior 2001 SCNIR estimate of ~8%/year (range 4–12%/year) MDS/AML hazard after year 10–12 — superseded by this report's 2.3%/year plateau; conflict preserved, not averaged.
supports: Severe congenital neutropenia is diagnosed clinically by absolute neutrophil counts persistently below 0.5x10^9/L with marrow maturation arrest of neutrophil precursors (clm_033; supports the unit-normalization inference clm_inf01).

sources.title: Outcomes of Isolated Neutropenia Referred to Pediatric Hematology-Oncology Clinic
limitations: single-center retrospective cohort
referral-population selection bias (peds heme-onc referrals only)
severity-band scheme is this study's operational categorization, not a consensus standard
conflictsWith: Severity-band cutoffs are not universally standardized; other pediatric sources define neutropenia severity bands differently (e.g., varying ANC breakpoints), so these exact bands should not be treated as the single authoritative standard.
supports: Black children had higher odds of persistent mild neutropenia in a pediatric hematology-oncology referral cohort, consistent with a benign ethnic neutropenia consideration (clm_074; direct basis for dec_cbc_benign_neutropenia_differential_pattern_001).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Pediatric isolated neutropenia referral-outcomes study update after 2020

sources.title: Outcomes for patients with severe chronic neutropenia treated with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor
limitations: Registry enrollment criteria, not a formal age-banded reference-interval study
No sex- or gestational/postnatal-age-specific ANC cutoffs given
BM examination timing/reading not standardized; genetic testing incomplete
ANC assay method (analyzer vs manual differential) not stated
supports: Congenital neutropenia was defined by early-childhood onset and/or genetic results with ANC remaining below 0.5x10^9/L beyond age 0.25 to 1.0 years (clm_004; direct basis for dec_cbc_marrow_red_flag_001).

MDS/AML occurred in 4.4% of all SCNIR patients (77/1752) but in 11.3% of the 670 congenital-neutropenia patients, concentrating myeloid risk in the congenital etiology (clm_005; direct basis for dec_cbc_marrow_red_flag_001).
No autoimmune/idiopathic-group patient (0 of 816) evolved to a myeloid malignancy, supporting a favorable myeloid prognosis for that etiology (clm_006; supports the marrow-risk inference clm_inf04).
Severe chronic neutropenia (SCN) is defined as blood neutrophils below 0.5x10^9/L persisting for more than 3 months (clm_001; supports the unit-normalization inference clm_inf01).

sources.title: Neutropenia in Childhood—A Narrative Review and Practical Diagnostic Approach
limitations: superscript exponent lost in extraction; unit reconstructed
narrative review — thresholds are secondary/derived not primary lab-derived
gene names ELANE/HAX1 partly dropped in extraction of Section 5
supports: Neutropenia is defined by age- and race-specific ANC cutoffs: <2500x10^9/L in neonates/infants, <1500x10^9/L in toddlers/older children/adults, and 1000-1500x10^9/L lower limit in African American children (clm_018; direct basis for dec_cbc_young_infant_scope_abstention_001).
Neutropenia severity is graded mild (1000-1500x10^9/L), moderate (500-999x10^9/L), and severe (<500x10^9/L) by ANC (clm_019; supports the unit-normalization inference clm_inf01).

sources.title: Duffy-Null Phenotype-Associated Neutropenia is the Most Common Etiology for Leukopenia/Neutropenia Referrals to a Tertiary Children's Hospital
supports: Duffy-null phenotype-associated neutropenia accounted for 77.7% of leukopenia/neutropenia referrals at a Detroit tertiary children's hospital (clm_039; direct basis for dec_cbc_benign_neutropenia_differential_pattern_001).
Among Yemeni pediatric referrals, Duffy-null phenotype-associated neutropenia prevalence was 96.6% (clm_040; direct basis for dec_cbc_benign_neutropenia_differential_pattern_001).
Among African American pediatric referrals, Duffy-null phenotype-associated neutropenia prevalence was 91% (clm_041; direct basis for dec_cbc_benign_neutropenia_differential_pattern_001).

Absence of recurrent, frequent, or serious infection is named as the discriminator between benign and pathologic neutropenia (clm_043; direct basis for dec_cbc_benign_neutropenia_differential_pattern_001).

sources.title: Cytogenetic findings in Polish patients with suspected Fanconi anemia
sources.organization: Advances in Clinical and Experimental Medicine (Wroclaw Medical University)
limitations: No explicit numeric break/cell cutoff for FA vs mosaic vs non-FA in the passages retrieved
Cohort spans age at diagnosis 1 month to 32 years — not purely pediatric
Single-center/national cohort, Polish population
supports: Mean mitomycin C (MMC)-induced chromosome breaks per cell were far higher in FA patients (6.67 ±3.92) than in non-FA patients (0.23 ±0.18). (clm_025).
Of 100 children with suspected FA, 13 (13%) had a true FA cellular phenotype, 27 had possible mosaic FA, and the remainder were classified non-FA. (clm_026).
Lymphocyte cultures were incubated for 72 h with MMC at 0, 50, 150, or 300 nM to induce clastogenic breakage. (clm_027).
The percentage of cells with spontaneous (baseline, non-MMC) chromosome aberrations was more than 9 times higher in FA than in non-FA patients. (clm_028).

The MMC sensitivity test discriminates FA, possible somatic mosaicism, and non-FA marrow failure at the first diagnostic step, but definitive diagnosis still requires follow-up mutation testing and fibroblast chromosome breakage analysis. (clm_029).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Fanconi anemia cytogenetic (MMC chromosome-breakage) diagnostic cohort update after 2024

sources.title: Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation for the Treatment of Severe Aplastic Anemia: Evidence-Based Guidelines From the American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy
sources.organization: Transplantation and Cellular Therapy (American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy)
sources.journal: ASTCT SAA HCT guideline (Transplant Cell Ther 2024;30(12):1155-1170)
limitations: Full-text PDF paywalled; graded statement wording verified via Guideline Central digest of the primary guideline, not the primary PDF.
HCT-specific scope only; does not cover non-transplant SAA diagnosis/monitoring thresholds.
Age thresholds are adult-facing eligibility criteria; guideline provides no numeric ATG dose or graft cell-dose cutoffs.
supports: The ASTCT panel preferentially recommends bone marrow (not peripheral blood) as the graft source for allogeneic HCT in severe aplastic anemia. (clm_049).
The panel recommends rabbit ATG over horse ATG as part of the HCT conditioning regimen for severe aplastic anemia. (clm_050).
The panel does not endorse the historical 40-year age cutoff for upfront HCT in adults with SAA, holding that fit older patients may also benefit. (clm_051).
The panel positively frames upfront HCT / MRD-HCT eligibility as extending up to 50 years of age or beyond for suitable patients, replacing the historical 40-year threshold. (clm_052).
Fludarabine-containing conditioning regimens are preferred for patients at high risk of graft failure and for matched-unrelated or haploidentical donor transplants. (clm_053).
For children and adults lacking a matched related donor, the panel prioritizes matched-unrelated or haploidentical donor HCT over immunosuppressive therapy. (clm_054).
sources.surveillanceQuery: ASTCT allogeneic HCT for severe aplastic anemia guideline update after 2024

sources.title: Diagnosis and management of acquired aplastic anemia in childhood. Guidelines from the Marrow Failure Study Group of the Pediatric Haemato-Oncology Italian Association (AIEOP)
sources.organization: Elsevier (Blood Cells, Molecules & Diseases, vol. 108, article 102860)
sources.journal: AIEOP Marrow Failure Study Group consensus, 2024
limitations: Full text not open-access (isOpenAccess=N, not in PMC/EPMC); severity-classification thresholds not extractable
National (Italian) consensus scope
supports: Acquired aplastic anemia incidence is 2-3 per million population per year in the Western world and about three times higher in East Asia. (clm_037).
Acquired aplastic anemia is defined as pancytopenia with a hypoplastic bone marrow, framing the diagnostic construct for the guideline. (clm_038).
The guideline states that in severe aplastic anemia, matched-sibling-donor HSCT is the first-line treatment (reported as guideline content, not an executable directive). (clm_039).
If no matched sibling donor is available, the guideline lists immunosuppressive therapy, matched-unrelated-donor HSCT, or haploidentical HSCT as options (reported as guideline content). (clm_040).
The document is an AIEOP Bone Marrow Failure Study Group evidence-based consensus practice guideline, developed through consensus conferences and validated by the AIEOP Board of Directors. (clm_041).
sources.surveillanceQuery: AIEOP pediatric acquired aplastic anemia guideline update after 2024

sources.title: Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of adult aplastic anaemia: A British Society for Haematology Guideline
sources.organization: British Journal of Haematology (British Society for Haematology)
limitations: Scope is ADULT acquired aplastic anaemia; the guideline does not define pediatric age-specific CBC reference intervals
Camitta severity cutoffs are fixed absolute-count disease-severity thresholds applied irrespective of patient age — they are NOT age/sex-adjusted reference intervals
Paywalled version of record (Wiley); quotes sourced from the accepted-manuscript .docx which may differ typographically from the final published copy-edit
supports: AA diagnosis requires pancytopenia with hypocellular marrow plus at least two of three peripheral-count thresholds (Hb, platelets, neutrophils). (clm_061).
Severe AA (Camitta) is defined by marrow cellularity <25% (or 25–50% with <30% residual haematopoiesis) plus at least two of neutrophils <0.5, platelets <20, reticulocytes <60 ×10⁹/L. (clm_062).
Very severe AA is defined identically to SAA except the neutrophil threshold is lowered to <0.2 ×10⁹/L. (clm_063).

The Camitta reticulocyte severity criterion has been modified from manual percentage to an absolute count <60 ×10⁹/L when measured by automated analyzers, which over-estimate reticulocytes relative to manual counts. (clm_064).
Where marrow cellularity is patchy, an overall average cellularity of <30% (excluding lymphocytes and plasma cells) should be ascertained to support the AA diagnosis. (clm_065).

AA incidence shows a biphasic age distribution with a first peak at 10–25 years (relevant to older children/adolescents) and a second peak over 60 years; incidence is 2–3 per million per year in Europe. (clm_066).

Given the rarity and management complexity of AA, the guideline strongly recommends multidisciplinary discussion and involvement of expert centres to improve outcomes — a referral/escalation trigger. (clm_067).
sources.surveillanceQuery: British Society for Haematology adult aplastic anaemia guideline update after 2024

sources.title: Disease progression and clinical outcomes in telomere biology disorders
limitations: Single-registry referral cohort (NCI IBMFS) — possible ascertainment/selection bias toward more severe disease
Reports epidemiological survival/prevalence outcomes, NOT laboratory assay reference intervals or CBC thresholds
Median survival estimates are cohort-level, not individual pediatric decision cutoffs
supports: The cohort comprised 231 individuals with dyskeratosis congenita/telomere biology disorders, 144 male, median age at diagnosis 19.4 years (range 0-71.6). (clm_030).
At last follow-up 42% of patients were deceased, with a median overall survival of 52.8 years (95% CI 45.5-57.6). (clm_031).
Transplant-free (HCT or solid-organ) median survival was 45.3 years, and overall survival was significantly better for autosomal-dominant vs AR/XLR/TINF2 disease (P<.01). (clm_032).
Severe bone marrow failure developed in 111 (48.1%) patients, and 70 (30.3%) underwent HCT at a median age of 15.4 years (range 0.9-63.1). (clm_033).
After adjustment for sex and pediatric-vs-adult diagnosis, AR/XLR patients had a hazard ratio for any cancer of 8 (95% CI 1.93-33.51) versus autosomal-dominant disease. (clm_034).
Median age at diagnosis differed sharply by inheritance mode: AD 36.1 years, AR/XLR 11.3 years, and TINF2 8.1 years. (clm_035).
Pulmonary fibrosis developed predominantly in adults with autosomal-dominant disease (19.6%), versus 9.5% in AR/XLR (P=.09) and 8% in TINF2 (P=.25). (clm_036).
sources.surveillanceQuery: telomere biology disorders disease progression and outcomes update after 2022

sources.title: Modified Delphi panel consensus recommendations for management of severe aplastic anemia
limitations: US-only 11-expert panel; may not generalize internationally
Consensus method (not GRADE evidence grading)
Absolute count cutoffs apply across all ages — NOT pediatric age/sex-specific reference intervals
supports: Severe aplastic anemia (SAA) is defined by marrow cellularity <25% (or 25%-50% with <30% residual hematopoietic cells) plus at least 2 of: neutrophils <0.5 x10^9/L, platelets <20 x10^9/L, reticulocytes <60 x10^9/L. (clm_055).
Very severe aplastic anemia fulfills the same criteria as SAA except that neutrophils are <0.2 x10^9/L. (clm_056).
A complete hematologic response is defined as neutrophils >1 x10^9/L (1.5 x10^9/L in some trials), platelets >100 x10^9/L, and Hgb >100 g/L. (clm_057).

A PNH granulocyte clone >1% has ~100% positive predictive value for acquired AA; a clone >0.1% has ~95% specificity and 91% PPV, while clones <0.1% are less specific and seen in confirmed IBMF. (clm_058).

For IBMF exclusion, patients aged <=40 years are recommended to have chromosome breakage analysis (Fanconi anemia) and telomere length analysis (telomere biology disorders); age 41-60 case-by-case; >60 with prior normal counts not recommended. (clm_059).

Descriptive supportive-care thresholds stated by the source: RBC transfusion at Hgb <7 g/L (verbatim; probable source typo for 7 g/dL / 70 g/L) or symptomatic anemia, and platelet transfusion at <10 x10^9/L (or <50 x10^9/L if bleeding or planned procedure). (clm_060).
sources.surveillanceQuery: modified Delphi consensus severe aplastic anemia diagnostic/response criteria update after 2024

sources.title: Long-term outcomes of patients with refractory cytopenia of childhood under observation only
limitations: Single observational cohort, normal-karyotype RCC only

PMC full-text extraction had dropped superscript '9' on the published ×10^9/L units; affected count-unit quotes re-verified against publisher JATS full text (Europe PMC PMC12395045) and corrected to the intact '× 10⁹/L' form
supports: In this observed RCC cohort, 84% (84/100) presented with a hypocellular diagnostic bone marrow, consistent with the typical RCC presentation. (clm_001).

EWOG-MDS assigned early HSCT (scope-exit from observation) for RCC patients with transfusion dependency, ANC <1×10^9/L, monosomy 7, del(7q), or >=2 chromosomal aberrations; others were observed. (clm_002).
The authors categorized 'significant' blood-count abnormalities during follow-up as Hb <10 g/dL, ANC <1×10^9/L, and/or platelets <100×10^9/L. (clm_003).
The authors categorized 'mild' blood-count abnormalities during follow-up as Hb 10-12 g/dL, ANC 1.0-1.5×10^9/L, platelets 100-150×10^9/L, and/or persistently high MCV. (clm_004).
For observed normal-karyotype RCC without predisposition, 5- and 10-year transplant(HSCT)-free survival were 94% and 88%, with all patients alive at median 7.2-year follow-up. (clm_005).

Given low rates of clonal evolution/PNH, the authors state yearly bone-marrow exams can be omitted; peripheral blood counts every 3-6 months, with marrow study only if counts deviate from baseline. (clm_006).
Patients who later underwent HSCT had lower median diagnosis-time Hb (8.7 vs 10.6 g/dL) and platelets (30 vs 64 ×10^9/L) than those never transplanted. (clm_007).
Cohort median age at diagnosis was 10.9 years (range 1.4-17.3), slight male predominance (M/F 1.2); age-adjusted MCV was increased in ~half of patients. (clm_008).
sources.surveillanceQuery: refractory cytopenia of childhood long-term observation-only outcomes update after 2025

sources.title: Diagnostic Value of a Protocolized In-Depth Evaluation of Pediatric Bone Marrow Failure: A Multi-Center Prospective Cohort Study
limitations: Small cohort (n=50)
Netherlands academic centers only
No causative diagnosis in 60%
Cutoffs are enrollment/analysis criteria not population reference intervals
supports: Study enrollment defined neutropenia for suspected pediatric BMF as an absolute neutrophil count below 1.5 x10^9/L. (clm_068).
Study enrollment defined thrombocytopenia for suspected pediatric BMF as a platelet count below 150 x10^9/L. (clm_069).
In the WES variant-interpretation pipeline, sequence variants with a gnomAD population allele frequency above 5% were excluded from further interpretation. (clm_070).
A protocolized in-depth evaluation established a causative diagnosis in 40% (20/50) of pediatric BMF patients, 18 of them by genetic analysis (14 mutations, 4 chromosomal deletions). (clm_071).

The protocol's first diagnostic step is exclusion of malignant and transient/reversible causes of BMF — patients are screened for leukemia and referred to a pediatric oncology center if indicated — before extended genetic/functional workup begins. (clm_072).

The authors recommend full genome-wide genetic analysis (WES with CNV plus SNP-array) complemented by functional tests in all suspected BMF patients, because causative genetic defects occur even without typical syndromic features. (clm_073).
Median peripheral-blood NK-cell percentage of total lymphocytes was significantly lower in the SAA group than in the identified-cause (constitutional) group (3.0 vs 9.0; P<0.05). (clm_074).
The primary WES gene filter (BMF gene panel) contained 172 genes plus HPO-term-driven selection, expanded to PID genes / open-WES / Trio-WES when primary analysis was negative. (clm_075).

sources.title: Diagnosis and Management of Acquired Aplastic Anemia: Consensus Statement of Indian Academy of Pediatrics
limitations: Full text paywalled (Springer/Indian Pediatrics); only the PubMed abstract recommendations were locatable verbatim
No PMC full text available — laboratory severity-count criteria (severe/very-severe ANC, platelet, reticulocyte cutoffs) could not be located in an accessible verbatim passage
India-specific consensus; resource-availability caveats may not generalize
supports: The IAP consensus states a bone marrow biopsy is mandatory to establish a diagnosis of acquired aplastic anemia in children. (clm_013).

Before diagnosing acquired AA, the consensus requires excluding inherited bone marrow failure syndromes, connective tissue disorders, viral infections, PNH, and drug/heavy-metal marrow suppression in all cases. (clm_014).
For pediatric AA, the consensus recommends a conservative transfusion approach targeting hemoglobin above 6 g/dL in children with no co-morbidities. (clm_015).
After immunosuppressive therapy, the consensus advises waiting 3-6 months before assessing response and considering next-line therapy. (clm_016).

The consensus names HLA-matched sibling donor HSCT as the preferred first-line treatment for newly diagnosed very severe/severe AA, and horse ATG plus cyclosporine as the recommended immunosuppressive therapy. (clm_017).

sources.title: Diagnosis, treatment, and surveillance of Diamond-Blackfan anaemia syndrome: international consensus statement
limitations: Level C evidence only; several thresholds are expert-consensus not trial-derived
Some numeric ranges reported without full consensus (noted per point)
supports: The consensus sets the acceptable haemoglobin target for DBA syndrome patients of all ages at >=9-10 g/dL, individualized to keep the patient asymptomatic. (clm_042).

Erythrocyte adenosine deaminase (eADA) activity helps differentiate DBA syndrome from other anaemias/IBMFS with 84% sensitivity and 95% specificity, but must be measured pre-transfusion and a normal value does not rule out DBA. (clm_043).

The target liver iron concentration (LIC), measured by MRI T2*/R2 (FerriScan), for transfusion-dependent DBA syndrome patients is as close to normal as possible, i.e., lower than 3 mg Fe/g dry weight. (clm_044).

Cardiac iron by MRI T2* should stay as close to normal as possible with an acceptable range of 20-35 msec; T2* <20 ms associates with decreased cardiac function/worse survival and <10 ms with imminent heart-failure risk. (clm_045).

Panel lowered the prednisone maintenance ceiling to a maximum of 0.3 mg/kg/day; if Hb >=9 g/dL cannot be sustained at <=0.3 mg/kg/day, steroids should be discontinued (some panelists proposed a stricter 0.2 mg/kg/day, no consensus). (clm_046).
Iron chelation is generally initiated after ~10 transfusions or evidence of iron load defined as ferritin >500 ng/mL, transferrin saturation >60%, or elevated LIC. (clm_047).

Classic DBA presents with macrocytic anaemia and reticulocytopenia, with 90% of patients becoming symptomatic in the first year of life at a median age of 3 months (though presentation can range from in-utero hydrops to the sixth decade). (clm_048).

sources.title: Integrated proteogenomic analysis for inherited bone marrow failure syndrome
limitations: Small per-disorder discovery cohort (SDS n=6, ADH5/ALDH2 n=4)
Multicenter stored specimens; protein-mRNA correlation low
Assay cutoffs are GAPDH-normalized MS ratios, not a standardized clinical assay
SBDS/ADH5 numeric cutoff magnitudes were exponent-stripped in the retrieved full text and are not reliably transcribable
supports: In 417 samples, the targeted-proteomic SBDS screen had 85.7% sensitivity / 93.4% specificity and the ADH5 screen 100.0% sensitivity / 97.5% specificity for SDS and ADH5/ALDH2 deficiency respectively. (clm_018).
The SBDS and ADH5 protein-expression cutoffs were defined as the cohort mean minus two standard deviations, computed on the 417 samples excluding confirmed SDS and ADH5/ALDH2 cases. (clm_019).

Six of six SDS patients showed significantly decreased SBDS protein, and two of them were not diagnosed by DNA sequencing alone (monoallelic on short-read DNA-seq, second variant recovered by RNA-seq). (clm_020).
The discovery cohort of 60 IBMFS patients had a median age at disease onset of 3 years (range 0-23) and included 27 men. (clm_021).

The study's 'IBMFS not otherwise specified' operational definition uses early onset (<2 years) and short telomere length (<2.0 SD) among its clinical criteria when no IBMFS pathogenic variant is found. (clm_022).
SDS carries a 15-30% risk of progression to myelodysplastic syndrome and acute myeloid leukemia, motivating early identification. (clm_023).

The ADH5/ALDH2-deficiency-associated ALDH2 variant (c.1510G>A, p.E504K [rs671]) is a common SNP present in 25.5% of East Asians, so ADH5 proteomics should be paired with genotyping in Asian IBMFS work-ups. (clm_024).

sources.title: Treatment of newly diagnosed severe aplastic anemia in children: Evidence-based recommendations
sources.organization: Pediatric Blood & Cancer (Wiley); Pediatr Blood Cancer 2024;71(8):e31070
limitations: Full text behind Wiley paywall; only the abstract was retrievable verbatim (PubMed).
Treatment-recommendations paper, not a laboratory reference-interval source — no lab reference intervals to extract.
Numeric diagnostic thresholds (modified Camitta SAA/vSAA criteria) are referenced by the panel but were NOT locatable in accessible text; recorded as GAPs, not asserted.
supports: Severe aplastic anemia (SAA) is a rare, potentially fatal hematologic disorder for which overall treatment outcomes are excellent, though management approaches vary and differ between adult and pediatric patients. (clm_009).
Certain aspects of pediatric SAA treatment remain under active investigation in clinical trials, so recommendations are not fully settled. (clm_010).
Because SAA is rare, some pediatric hematologists have relatively limited experience with its complex management — the rationale for these consolidated recommendations. (clm_011).
The panel's recommendations are framed as an up-to-date, evidence-based approach specifically for children with newly diagnosed SAA. (clm_012).
sources.surveillanceQuery: pediatric severe aplastic anemia evidence-based treatment recommendations update after 2024
